code: var preferences = await ask(

cuisineKnowledge.display:
{ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "japanese": "# Japanese Cuisine

Japanese cooking is defined by precision, respect for ingredients, and the pursuit of umami — the fifth taste.

## Key Principles
- **Umami layering** — Build depth by combining dashi, soy sauce, miso, and mirin in careful ratios.
- **Shun (旬)** — Cook with ingredients at their seasonal peak. The menu follows the calendar.
- **Presentation** — Food is arranged with intention. Odd numbers, negative space, and color contrast are valued.
- **Knife work** — Clean, precise cuts affect texture, cooking time, and appearance equally.

## Essential Techniques
- **Dashi making** — Steep kombu and bonito flakes briefly for a clean, umami-rich stock. Never boil the kombu.
- **Tempura frying** — Ice-cold batter, high heat (170–180°C), small batches. Lumps in the batter are fine — overmixing makes it heavy.
- **Rice washing** — Rinse until the water runs clear, then soak 30 minutes before cooking.
- **Teriyaki glaze** — Equal parts soy sauce, mirin, and sake, reduced until glossy.

## Staple Ingredients
Short-grain rice, soy sauce, mirin, sake, rice vinegar, miso paste, kombu, bonito flakes, nori, sesame oil, wasabi, ginger.

## Classic Dishes
Sushi, ramen, tempura, tonkatsu, miso soup, okonomiyaki, gyudon, matcha desserts.", "italian": "# Italian Cuisine

Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.

## Key Principles
- **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity.
- **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta.
- **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time.
- **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.

## Essential Techniques
- **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating.
- **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream.
- **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.

## Staple Ingredients
Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.

## Classic Dishes
Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu.", "mexican": "# Mexican Cuisine

Mexican cooking is one of the world's great culinary traditions — built on indigenous ingredients, bold chiles, and techniques refined over millennia.

## Key Principles
- **Chile complexity** — Different chiles serve different roles: ancho for sweetness, chipotle for smoke, habanero for heat. Toast dried chiles before rehydrating.
- **Nixtamalization** — Treating corn with lime (cal) transforms it into masa, the foundation of tortillas and tamales. This ancient technique unlocks niacin and changes the flavor profile.
- **Layered seasoning** — Flavors are built in stages: toast, char, blend, simmer.
- **Low and slow** — Barbacoa, birria, and carnitas all rely on long, gentle cooking to break down tough cuts.

## Essential Techniques
- **Salsa making** — Char tomatoes and chiles on a comal or under a broiler before blending. The char adds smoky depth.
- **Tortilla pressing** — Masa should be the texture of Play-Doh. Press thin, cook on a dry comal at high heat, and look for the puff.
- **Mole building** — Toast chiles, nuts, seeds, and spices separately, then blend and simmer for hours. Patience is the key ingredient.

## Staple Ingredients
Corn masa, dried chiles (ancho, guajillo, chipotle), black beans, tomatillos, cilantro, lime, avocado, queso fresco, epazote, Mexican oregano.

## Classic Dishes
Tacos al pastor, mole poblano, pozole, tamales, chiles rellenos, elote, churros." } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": "# Sauté

High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.

## When to Use
- Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus)
- Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops)
- Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing

## Key Technique
1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke.
2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed.
3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning.
4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.

## Equipment
A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.

## Common Mistakes
- Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing)
- Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils)
- Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)", "braise": "# Braise

Braising combines dry heat (searing) and wet heat (simmering in liquid) for dishes that are deeply flavorful and fall-apart tender.

## When to Use
- Tough, collagen-rich cuts of meat (short ribs, pork shoulder, lamb shanks, chuck roast)
- Root vegetables that benefit from long cooking (carrots, turnips, parsnips)
- Any dish where you want rich, concentrated sauce built from the cooking liquid

## Key Technique
1. **Sear first** — Pat the protein dry, season generously, and sear in hot oil until deeply browned on all sides. This is where most of the flavor comes from.
2. **Build aromatics** — Sauté onions, carrots, celery, and garlic in the same pot after searing.
3. **Deglaze** — Add wine, stock, or other liquid and scrape up the fond (browned bits).
4. **Submerge halfway** — The liquid should come about halfway up the protein, not cover it completely.
5. **Low and slow** — Cover and cook at 150–165°C (300–325°F) for 2–4 hours until fork-tender.
6. **Rest and reduce** — Remove the protein, strain and reduce the braising liquid into a sauce.

## Equipment
A heavy Dutch oven with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron or enameled cast iron retains heat evenly.

## Common Mistakes
- Skipping the sear (you lose enormous flavor)
- Cooking at too high a temperature (the protein toughens before the collagen breaks down)
- Not reducing the braising liquid (thin, watery sauce instead of a rich glaze)", "grill": "# Grill

Grilling uses direct radiant heat from below to cook food quickly, creating characteristic char marks and smoky flavor.

## When to Use
- Steaks, burgers, chops, and other quick-cooking proteins
- Vegetables with enough structure to hold together (corn, zucchini, peppers, eggplant)
- Flatbreads, pizza, and fruit (peaches, pineapple)

## Key Technique
1. **Preheat thoroughly** — 15–20 minutes with the lid closed. The grates should be too hot to hold your hand near for more than 2 seconds.
2. **Clean and oil the grates** — Brush with a grill brush, then oil with a folded paper towel dipped in high-smoke-point oil.
3. **Two-zone setup** — Pile coals on one side (or turn on only half the burners) for a hot sear zone and a cooler indirect zone.
4. **Don't press** — Pressing burgers or chicken squeezes out juices. Let the food sit until it releases naturally.
5. **Rest the meat** — Rest for 5–10 minutes after grilling. Internal temperature will rise 3–5°F during resting.

## Temperature Guide
- **High (230°C+ / 450°F+)** — Steaks, thin cuts, vegetables
- **Medium (175–230°C / 350–450°F)** — Chicken pieces, fish, sausages
- **Low indirect (120–150°C / 250–300°F)** — Whole chickens, ribs, large roasts

## Common Mistakes
- Opening the lid too often (loses heat and extends cooking time)
- Only using direct heat (thick cuts burn outside before cooking through)
- Not letting the grill preheat (food sticks and doesn't char properly)" } } } }

plan.description: Creates a weekly meal plan with balanced nutrition, grocery list, and prep schedule

tasks.instructions: Determine the user's meal planning preferences for the week. Load relevant cuisine and dietary knowledge to understand their restrictions and preferences. Consider: number of people, meals per day, cooking skill level, time available on weekdays vs weekends, budget level, and any nutritional goals (e.g., high protein, low carb, calorie target). Identify which cuisines and techniques the user is comfortable with.

mealsPerDay.description: Number of meals to plan per day

weekdayCookingTime.description: Available cooking time on weekdays (e.g., '30 minutes')

weekendCookingTime.description: Available cooking time on weekends (e.g., '1-2 hours')

calorieTarget.description: Daily calorie target per person, if specified

tasks.instructions: Design a complete weekly menu based on the user's preferences. Balance variety across cuisines, techniques, and protein sources throughout the week. Assign quicker meals to busy weekdays and more involved recipes to weekends. Plan for leftovers where practical — a weekend roast can become weekday sandwiches or salads. Ensure nutritional balance across the week. Use the estimateCalories function to validate daily calorie totals.

isLeftover.description: Whether this uses leftovers from a previous meal

dailyCalories.description: Total estimated calories for the day

weeklyStrategy.description: Overview of the meal plan strategy (variety, leftovers, prep batching)

tasks.instructions: Create an efficient prep schedule that batches work to reduce daily effort. Identify ingredients and components that can be prepared in advance — sauces, grains, chopped vegetables, marinated proteins. Organize into a weekend batch-prep session and daily quick-prep tasks. Use the formatTime function to present durations clearly. Include storage instructions and shelf life for prepped items.

day.description: When to do batch prep (e.g., 'Sunday afternoon')

storage.description: How to store (fridge, freezer, container type)

shelfLife.description: How long the prepped item keeps

tasks.instructions: Compile a complete grocery list for the weekly meal plan. Use the buildGroceryList function to deduplicate and consolidate ingredients across all planned meals. Organize items by store section for efficient shopping. Include quantities scaled for the number of people using scaleIngredients. Flag items that may already be in the pantry. Note any specialty items that may require a specific store.

isSpecialty.description: Whether this may need a specialty store

pantryCheck.description: Items likely already in the pantry — verify before buying

estimatedTotal.description: Rough cost estimate for the grocery list

output.weeklyStrategy: Japanese-focused meal plan with strategic leftovers. Weekdays feature quick 30-minute meals (rice bowls, miso soup, gyoza) using batch-prepped components. Weekend cooking includes longer recipes (okonomiyaki, katsu curry, ramen) that create delicious leftovers for busy days. Each day balances protein, vegetables, and rice while staying around 500 calories on weekdays, with more generous portions on weekends.

design-weekly-menu.weeklyStrategy: Japanese-focused meal plan with strategic leftovers. Weekdays feature quick 30-minute meals (rice bowls, miso soup, gyoza) using batch-prepped components. Weekend cooking includes longer recipes (okonomiyaki, katsu curry, ramen) that create delicious leftovers for busy days. Each day balances protein, vegetables, and rice while staying around 500 calories on weekdays, with more generous portions on weekends.
